Clustering S&P 500 Stocks with Machine Learning

ML Project 1

Author

Jake Kulik-Warren

Introduction

Traditional stock classifications — typically based on sectors, market capitalization, or other static attributes — may not fully capture the dynamic patterns embedded in stock return behavior. Brown and Goetzmann (1997)1 introduced an innovative, unsupervised approach to classify mutual funds based solely on their return profiles, challenging conventional, self-reported categorizations. Their work demonstrated that clustering fund returns could uncover distinct investment styles more reflective of underlying performance characteristics.

Motivated by Brown and Goetzmann’s methodology, this project extends the idea of return-based clustering from mutual funds to individual stocks. Instead of relying on predefined categories, we group stocks based on their historical return patterns, allowing us to identify latent clusters that reveal new insights into market behavior and stock performance.

Objectives

  1. Data Manipulation and Feature Engineering: Prepare data and construct return-based features (e.g., rolling cumulative returns) to capture the dynamic behavior of stock returns.
  2. Exploratory Analysis: Conduct EDA to understand distributions and key characteristics of the data using visualizations and summary statistics.
  3. Apply Unsupervised Learning: Use k-means clustering to classify stocks in a data-driven manner, free from traditional categorizations. Carefully determine the optimal number of clusters (K).
  4. Uncover Latent Clusters: Identify hidden groupings among stocks based solely on their historical return patterns.
  5. Reveal Novel Insights: Explore whether these clusters uncover new patterns or investment styles, utilizing effective visualizations and clear technical communication.

Setup

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(tidyquant)
library(h2o)
library(scales)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(corrplot)
library(slider)
library(moments)

# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(42)

Data Collection

We obtain the current S&P 500 constituents using tq_index() and then pull monthly adjusted closing prices from 2001 through 2024 using tq_get(). This long span captures various market cycles including the dot-com aftermath, the 2008 financial crisis, the COVID-19 crash, and the post-pandemic recovery.

Fetch S&P 500 data 
# Get current S&P 500 constituents
sp500 <- tq_index("SP500")

# Pull monthly stock prices (2001-2024)
stock_prices <- sp500 %>%
  pull(symbol) %>%
  tq_get(
    get  = "stock.prices",
    from = "2001-01-01",
    to   = "2024-12-31"
  )

# Compute monthly returns
monthly_returns <- stock_prices %>%
  group_by(symbol) %>%
  tq_transmute(
    select     = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = periodReturn,
    period     = "monthly",
    col_rename = "monthly_return"
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

cat("Unique stocks:", n_distinct(monthly_returns$symbol), "\n")
Unique stocks: 500
Fetch S&P 500 data 
cat("Date range:", as.character(min(monthly_returns$date)),
    "to", as.character(max(monthly_returns$date)), "\n")
Date range: 2001-01-31 to 2024-12-30
Fetch S&P 500 data 
cat("Total observations:", nrow(monthly_returns), "\n")
Total observations: 126931

Exploratory Data Analysis

Return Distribution

Code 
monthly_returns %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = monthly_return)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 150, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Monthly Returns (All S&P 500 Stocks)",
    subtitle = "2001-2024 | Red dashed line indicates zero return",
    x     = "Monthly Return",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(), limits = c(-0.5, 0.5)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray50"))

Summary Statistics

Code 
monthly_returns %>%
  summarise(
    `Mean`   = mean(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Median` = median(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Std Dev`  = sd(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Skewness` = moments::skewness(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Kurtosis` = moments::kurtosis(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Min`    = min(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Max`    = max(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE),
    `N`      = n()
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 4, caption = "Summary Statistics of Monthly Returns (2001-2024)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Summary Statistics of Monthly Returns (2001-2024)
Mean Median Std Dev Skewness Kurtosis Min Max N
0.0139 0.0132 0.0961 1.2533 26.3568 -0.8435 2.5966 126931

The positive mean return of approximately 1.4% per month is consistent with the long-run equity premium. The excess kurtosis indicates fat tails — extreme returns occur more frequently than a normal distribution would predict, which is a well-documented feature of stock returns.

Data Availability Per Stock

Code 
monthly_returns %>%
  count(symbol) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "darkorange", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 24, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = Inf, label = "Min 24 months\nrequired",
           vjust = 2, hjust = 0, size = 3, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Monthly Return Observations Per Stock",
    x     = "Number of Months",
    y     = "Number of Stocks"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Average Return by Year

Code 
monthly_returns %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(date)) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(avg_return = mean(monthly_return, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = avg_return, fill = avg_return > 0)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("red3", "forestgreen")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Monthly Return by Year",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Monthly Return"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Feature Engineering

Following the Brown and Goetzmann (1997) framework, we compute rolling 24-month features for each stock. These features capture the return dynamics over a two-year backward-looking window:

  • Rolling Cumulative Return: \(\prod_{i=1}^{24}(1+r_{t-i+1}) - 1\) — captures total performance over the window
  • Average Monthly Return: Arithmetic mean — captures central tendency
  • Volatility: Standard deviation — captures risk/variability
  • Skewness: Asymmetry of returns — captures tail risk direction
  • Kurtosis: Tail thickness — captures extreme event frequency
Compute rolling 24-month features 
window <- 24

features <- monthly_returns %>%
  arrange(symbol, date) %>%
  group_by(symbol) %>%
  mutate(
    rolling_cum_return = slider::slide_dbl(
      monthly_return,
      .f = ~ prod(1 + .x) - 1,
      .before = window - 1,
      .complete = TRUE
    ),
    rolling_avg_return = slider::slide_dbl(
      monthly_return,
      .f = ~ mean(.x),
      .before = window - 1,
      .complete = TRUE
    ),
    rolling_volatility = slider::slide_dbl(
      monthly_return,
      .f = ~ sd(.x),
      .before = window - 1,
      .complete = TRUE
    ),
    rolling_skewness = slider::slide_dbl(
      monthly_return,
      .f = ~ moments::skewness(.x),
      .before = window - 1,
      .complete = TRUE
    ),
    rolling_kurtosis = slider::slide_dbl(
      monthly_return,
      .f = ~ moments::kurtosis(.x),
      .before = window - 1,
      .complete = TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  drop_na(rolling_cum_return)

cat("Feature matrix rows:", nrow(features), "\n")
Feature matrix rows: 115468
Compute rolling 24-month features 
cat("Unique stocks with features:", n_distinct(features$symbol), "\n")
Unique stocks with features: 496

Feature Distributions

Code 
features %>%
  select(rolling_cum_return, rolling_avg_return,
         rolling_volatility, rolling_skewness, rolling_kurtosis) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(feature = str_replace_all(feature, "_", " ") %>% str_to_title()) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 80, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Rolling 24-Month Feature Distributions",
       x = "Value", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

Feature Correlations

Code 
cor_matrix <- features %>%
  select(rolling_cum_return, rolling_avg_return,
         rolling_volatility, rolling_skewness, rolling_kurtosis) %>%
  cor(use = "complete.obs")

corrplot::corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
                   addCoef.col = "black", tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45,
                   col = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(10, "RdBu"),
                   title = "Feature Correlation Matrix",
                   mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

Clustering with H2O

Prepare Clustering Data

We use the most recent observation per stock (the latest 24-month rolling window) to form our cross-sectional feature matrix for clustering. To avoid extreme outliers from dominating the clustering (e.g., stocks with 30x+ cumulative returns forming single-stock clusters), we winsorize features at the 1st and 99th percentiles before clustering.

Prepare and winsorize cross-sectional feature matrix 
# Extract latest observation per stock
cluster_data <- features %>%
  group_by(symbol) %>%
  filter(date == max(date)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(symbol, rolling_cum_return, rolling_avg_return,
         rolling_volatility, rolling_skewness, rolling_kurtosis) %>%
  drop_na()

# Winsorize function: cap extreme values at given percentiles
winsorize <- function(x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99)) {
  q <- quantile(x, probs, na.rm = TRUE)
  x[x < q[1]] <- q[1]
  x[x > q[2]] <- q[2]
  x
}

# Apply winsorization to clustering features
feature_cols <- c("rolling_cum_return", "rolling_avg_return",
                  "rolling_volatility", "rolling_skewness",
                  "rolling_kurtosis")

cluster_data_w <- cluster_data %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_cols), winsorize))

cat("Stocks in clustering sample:", nrow(cluster_data_w), "\n")
Stocks in clustering sample: 496

Initialize H2O and Run K-Means

Initialize H2O 
h2o.init(nthreads = -1, max_mem_size = "4g")

H2O is not running yet, starting it now...

Starting H2O JVM and connecting:  Connection successful!

    H2O cluster total cores:    24 
    H2O cluster allowed cores:  24

Initialize H2O 
h2o.no_progress()

# Upload winsorized data to H2O
cluster_h2o <- as.h2o(cluster_data_w)

Determine Optimal K (Elbow Method)

The elbow method plots the total within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) against the number of clusters. The optimal K is at the “elbow” point where adding more clusters yields diminishing reductions in WCSS.

Code 
k_range <- 2:12

elbow_results <- tibble(k = k_range) %>%
  mutate(
    model = map(k, ~ h2o.kmeans(
      training_frame = cluster_h2o,
      x              = feature_cols,
      k              = .x,
      standardize    = TRUE,
      seed           = 42,
      max_iterations = 100
    )),
    tot_withinss = map_dbl(model, ~ h2o.tot_withinss(.x))
  )

# Calculate marginal improvement
elbow_plot_data <- elbow_results %>%
  mutate(
    pct_reduction = (lag(tot_withinss) - tot_withinss) / lag(tot_withinss)
  )

elbow_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = k, y = tot_withinss)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", alpha = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = 5.3, y = max(elbow_results$tot_withinss) * 0.9,
           label = "K = 5", color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Elbow Method for Optimal K",
    subtitle = "Total Within-Cluster Sum of Squares by Number of Clusters",
    x     = "Number of Clusters (K)",
    y     = "Total Within-Cluster SS"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = k_range) +
  theme_minimal()

Marginal WCSS Reduction

Code 
elbow_plot_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_reduction)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = k, y = pct_reduction)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = k_range) +
  labs(
    title = "Marginal WCSS Reduction by K",
    x = "K",
    y = "% Reduction from K-1"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Fit Final Model

Based on the elbow plot and marginal reduction analysis, we select K=5 as the optimal number of clusters.

Fit final K-means model with K=5 
optimal_k <- 5

final_model <- h2o.kmeans(
  training_frame = cluster_h2o,
  x              = feature_cols,
  k              = optimal_k,
  standardize    = TRUE,
  seed           = 42,
  max_iterations = 100
)

# Get cluster assignments
predictions <- h2o.predict(final_model, cluster_h2o) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rename(cluster = predict)

# Bind back to original (non-winsorized) data for interpretable results
cluster_results <- cluster_data %>%
  bind_cols(predictions) %>%
  mutate(cluster = factor(cluster))

cat("Cluster sizes:\n")
Cluster sizes:
Fit final K-means model with K=5 
print(table(cluster_results$cluster))

  0   1   2   3   4 
237  18 105 124  12

Results

Cluster Centers

Code 
# Compute cluster means from original data for interpretability
cluster_results %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    across(all_of(feature_cols), mean),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 4, caption = "Cluster Centers (Original Feature Scale)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Cluster Centers (Original Feature Scale)

1 1.3600 0.0410 0.1348 1.2810 5.3262
2 0.1118 0.0065 0.0924 0.5254 3.1879
3 1.0665 0.0332 0.0850 0.1824 2.5600
4 10.7674 0.1134 0.2163 0.7454 3.2746

  ggplot(aes(x = rolling_volatility, y = rolling_cum_return,

  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    title = "K-Means Clusters: Cumulative Return vs. Volatility",
    subtitle = "24-month rolling window | Most recent period per stock",
    x     = "Rolling Volatility (24-month)",
    y     = "Rolling Cumulative Return (24-month)",
    color = "Cluster"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray50"))

Cluster Visualization: Average Return vs. Skewness

Code 
cluster_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = rolling_skewness, y = rolling_avg_return,
             color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.3) +

    title = "K-Means Clusters: Average Return vs. Skewness",
    x     = "Rolling Skewness (24-month)",
    y     = "Average Monthly Return (24-month)",
    color = "Cluster"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")

Cluster Summary Statistics

Code 
cluster_results %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    `Count`    = n(),
    `Avg Cum Return` = mean(rolling_cum_return),
    `Avg Volatility` = mean(rolling_volatility),
    `Avg Monthly Return` = mean(rolling_avg_return),
    `Avg Skewness` = mean(rolling_skewness),
    `Avg Kurtosis` = mean(rolling_kurtosis),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 4, caption = "Cluster Summary Statistics") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

Cluster Profiles: Boxplots

Code 
cluster_results %>%
  select(cluster, all_of(feature_cols)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cluster, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(feature = str_replace_all(feature, "_", " ") %>% str_to_title()) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = cluster, y = value, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Feature Distributions by Cluster",
       x = "Cluster", y = "Value") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Cluster Interpretation

Generate cluster labels 
# Assign descriptive labels based on cluster characteristics
cluster_labels <- cluster_results %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    avg_cum = mean(rolling_cum_return),
    avg_vol = mean(rolling_volatility),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(avg_cum) %>%
  mutate(
    label = case_when(
      avg_cum < 0 ~ "Underperformers",
      avg_vol < quantile(avg_vol, 0.3) & avg_cum < quantile(avg_cum, 0.5) ~ "Low Vol / Moderate Return",
      avg_vol < quantile(avg_vol, 0.5) & avg_cum >= quantile(avg_cum, 0.5) ~ "Steady Growers",
      avg_vol >= quantile(avg_vol, 0.5) & avg_cum >= quantile(avg_cum, 0.7) ~ "High Growth / High Vol",
      TRUE ~ "Moderate"
    )
  )

cluster_labels %>%
  select(Cluster = cluster, Label = label, Count = n,
         `Avg Cum Return` = avg_cum, `Avg Volatility` = avg_vol) %>%
  kable(digits = 4, caption = "Cluster Interpretation") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Cluster Interpretation
Cluster Label Count Avg Cum Return Avg Volatility
2 Moderate 105 0.1118 0.0924
0 Low Vol / Moderate Return 237 0.1426 0.0677
3 Steady Growers 124 1.0665 0.0850
1 High Growth / High Vol 18 1.3600 0.1348
4 High Growth / High Vol 12 10.7674 0.2163

Example Stocks Per Cluster

Code 
sp500_info <- sp500 %>% select(symbol, company, sector, weight)

cluster_results %>%
  left_join(sp500_info, by = "symbol") %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  arrange(desc(rolling_cum_return)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(cluster, symbol, company, sector,
         rolling_cum_return, rolling_volatility) %>%
  mutate(
    rolling_cum_return = percent(rolling_cum_return, accuracy = 0.1),
    rolling_volatility = percent(rolling_volatility, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  kable(caption = "Top 5 Stocks Per Cluster (Ranked by Cumulative Return)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

Sector Composition by Cluster

Code 
cluster_results %>%
  left_join(sp500_info, by = "symbol") %>%
  count(cluster, sector) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = cluster, y = n, fill = sector)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  labs(
    title = "Sector Composition by Cluster",
    subtitle = "Return-based clusters do not perfectly align with sector classifications",
    x     = "Cluster",
    y     = "Proportion",
    fill  = "Sector"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 7),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray50"))

Sector-Level Cluster Distribution

Code 
cluster_results %>%
  left_join(sp500_info, by = "symbol") %>%
  count(sector, cluster) %>%
  group_by(sector) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = cluster, y = reorder(sector, pct), fill = pct)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct * 100), "%")), size = 3) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "steelblue",
                      labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Cluster Distribution Within Each Sector",
    x = "Cluster",
    y = "Sector",
    fill = "% of Sector"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Conclusion

This analysis applied k-means clustering to S&P 500 stocks using return-based features inspired by Brown and Goetzmann (1997). After winsorizing extreme outliers to prevent single-stock clusters, we identified 5 distinct clusters with meaningful economic interpretation.

Key findings:

  • Cluster differentiation: Stocks naturally group into distinct clusters based on their rolling cumulative return, volatility, and higher-order return moments — independent of traditional sector classifications.
  • Return-risk spectrum: The clusters range from underperforming, higher-volatility stocks to steady low-volatility growers and high-growth/high-volatility names, revealing the full spectrum of risk-return profiles present in the S&P 500.
  • Sector overlap: Traditional sector labels do not perfectly align with return-based clusters. For example, Technology stocks appear across multiple clusters, suggesting that return dynamics capture information beyond what sector classification alone provides.
  • Practical implications: Return-based clustering may assist portfolio construction by identifying groups of stocks with genuinely similar performance characteristics. A portfolio manager could use these clusters to ensure true diversification across return profiles rather than relying solely on sector allocations.

Challenges encountered:

  • Survivorship bias: We used current S&P 500 constituents, which may not match the historical index composition — stocks that were removed due to poor performance are excluded.
  • Outlier sensitivity: K-means is sensitive to extreme outliers; winsorization was necessary to produce balanced clusters. Alternative approaches like DBSCAN or k-medoids could be explored in future work.
  • K selection: The choice of K remains somewhat subjective; the elbow method provides guidance but not a definitive answer. Silhouette analysis or the gap statistic could provide additional validation.
  • Feature selection: The rolling window of 24 months is a modeling choice — shorter or longer windows would capture different aspects of stock behavior and could produce different cluster structures.

Footnotes

  1. Mutual Fund Styles, Brown and Goetzmann, 1997↩︎
